Also, the documentation was updated to clarify how to refresh the PlaceListMapTemplate content, and some minor formatting and wording changes.

entries.title: Build a point of interest app
entries.summary: The changes ensure that developers are using the correct and current categories for their POI apps, aligning with the latest Car App Library versions. The refresh content update helps developers implement content refresh functionality in their apps.

entries.title: Build a weather app
entries.summary: The documentation for weather apps has been updated to clarify the purpose of weather applications in cars, emphasizing their ability to provide weather information for current locations or along a route, and offering navigation capabilities.  The document also updates formatting, link structures and emphasis.

entries.title: Car ready mobile apps
entries.summary: This document outlines the Car ready mobile apps program, which allows eligible mobile apps in the video, games, and browser categories to be distributed to cars with Android Automotive OS. It details eligibility requirements, testing procedures, and how to participate and opt-out of the program. The document has been updated to clarify the launch date, the availability of apps, and the impact of the program on app distribution.

entries.title: Build communication apps for Android Auto
entries.summary: The document has been updated to use a more descriptive introductory paragraph and a more modern callout block for the tip. This change provides a better overview of communication features on Android Auto, improving readability and user experience.

entries.title: Build calling experiences for Android Auto
entries.summary: The documentation for calling apps has been updated to use the `> [!IMPORTANT]` syntax for important notes. This change improves the visual emphasis of the critical information for developers, making it more noticeable. Additionally, it clarifies the requirement to include both calling and messaging categories in the same intent filter if an app supports both features.

entries.title: Extend messaging notifications to Android Auto
entries.summary: The documentation for Android Auto messaging notifications has been updated. The changes include adding a description, and updating the formatting of "Important", "Warning", and "Tip" sections using the `> [!IMPORTANT]`, `> [!WARNING]`, and `> [!TIP]` syntax. These updates improve readability and clarify important information for developers integrating messaging features with Android Auto. The documentation also clarifies the use of `PendingIntent.FLAG_MUTABLE`.

entries.title: Build templated messaging experiences for Android Auto
entries.summary: The documentation for templated messaging has been updated to use the 'Note' and 'Tip' syntax using the markdown syntax. This change improves the visual clarity and formatting of the document for developers, making important information stand out more effectively.

entries.title: Distribute to cars
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of how to distribute Android apps for Android Auto and Android Automotive OS (AAOS). It outlines guidelines and requirements for different app categories to help developers successfully launch their apps on these platforms.

entries.title: Media apps for cars overview
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to reflect formatting changes, improved code examples, and more helpful content regarding the development of media apps for Android Auto and AAOS. Formatting and some wording were updated in several sections, including the section about creating audio media apps. The documentation has also added or updated some CAUTION and NOTE blocks.

entries.title: Add support for Android Auto to your media app
entries.summary: The documentation for Android Auto media support has been updated. The changes include a minor formatting adjustment to emphasize the key information regarding existing media browser service implementation by using a callout. This change aims to improve clarity and highlight the prerequisites for integrating media apps with Android Auto.

entries.title: Add Android Automotive OS support to your media app
entries.summary: The documentation for Android Automotive OS media apps was updated. Changes include rewording, improved formatting with admonitions, and clarifying the steps for project setup, particularly manifest configuration and Gradle dependencies.

entries.title: Configure manifest files
entries.summary: This document describes how to configure your app's manifest files to integrate with Android Auto and Android Automotive OS (AAOS), focusing on declaring the media browser service and specifying app icons, labels, and accent colors.  Changes include minor formatting adjustments, clarifying descriptions, and updating code snippets for better readability.

entries.title: Media browser service overview
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of creating a media browser service for Android Auto and Android Automotive OS (AAOS). The primary change involves clarifying the classes to extend (MediaBrowserServiceCompat or MediaLibraryService) and how Android Auto/AAOS utilizes the service for content browsing and media playback control. This matters because it provides essential context for developers building media applications for car platforms, ensuring compatibility and proper functionality within the Android Auto/AAOS environment.

entries.title: Display browsable search results
entries.summary: This document describes how to enhance the search experience in Android Auto and Android Automotive OS by enabling searches without voice and displaying search results. The changes include clarifying the purpose of the page, refining the descriptions of how to support search, and updating formatting.

entries.title: Build your content hierarchy
entries.summary: This document introduces updates to the content hierarchy implementation for Android Auto and Android Automotive OS (AAOS) media browser services. The changes include clarification and better formatting of code snippets, and updated package validation guidance including allowing Google Assistant access to the MediaBrowserService. It also highlights the importance of root menu structure, tab limits, and the use of root hints to optimize content display.

entries.title: Apply content styles
entries.summary: This document introduces changes related to content styles in Android Automotive OS (AAOS) media applications. It clarifies how to apply content styles for browsable and playable items, including setting defaults using BrowserRoot extras and overriding styles for specific items or their descendants. The updates also provide clearer guidance on grouping media items using title hints, emphasizing the importance of contiguous blocks for correct grouping and explaining how the order of items impacts the grouping logic.

entries.title: Implement custom browse actions
entries.summary: This document describes how to implement custom browse actions in an Android MediaBrowserServiceCompat for Android Auto. It explains how to define actions, attach them to MediaItems, handle user interactions, and update action states. The document has been updated with minor improvements to clarify the implementation steps, and formatting.

entries.title: Media browser service lifecycle
entries.summary: This document describes the interaction between Android Automotive OS (AAOS) and Android Auto with a media browser service. The changes improve formatting, clarity, and readability by adding emphasis and fixing minor issues.

entries.title: Display media artwork
entries.summary: The documentation updates the formatting of the warning and note sections by replacing the HTML-style formatting with the markdown-style formatting. This change improves the visual presentation and readability of the documentation.

entries.title: Implement distraction safeguards
entries.summary: This document explains how to implement distraction safeguards in Android Auto media apps. It focuses on preventing automatic audio playback, such as alarms, and managing how advertisements are displayed to minimize driver distraction. The document provides instructions on using the `CarConnection` API and setting the `METADATA_KEY_IS_ADVERTISEMENT` key.

entries.title: Enable playback control
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to use the `> [!NOTE]` syntax for callout boxes instead of the previous `| **Note:**` format for note sections. This change improves the visual consistency and clarity of the documentation.

entries.title: Handle errors
entries.summary: The documentation for handling errors in Android Auto and Android Automotive has been updated to clarify how error messages are displayed and how to address errors. It provides additional guidance on providing user-facing and localized error messages, including a specific recommendation to instruct users to interact with their phone if necessary. The changes improve clarity and provide more specific guidance on implementing error handling in media applications for cars.

entries.title: Support voice actions
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on integrating voice actions into Android Auto and Android Automotive OS (AAOS) media apps. The changes mainly focus on improving readability and clarity, including formatting code, adding a note, and refining the explanation of how voice actions are handled by apps.

entries.title: Build parked apps for cars
entries.summary: The documentation for parked apps has been updated to include information about support for different display sizes, and more emphasis on app distribution. The changes provide more guidance on how to optimize apps for cars and how to distribute them through Google Play.

entries.title: Add support for Android Auto to your parked app
entries.summary: This document introduces changes related to Android Auto app development. The updates include enhanced formatting, added warnings and tips using the `[!CAUTION]`, `[!IMPORTANT]`, and `[!TIP]` callouts, and clarified guidance on supporting different screen sizes and detecting Android Auto usage.

entries.title: Add support for Android Automotive OS to your parked app
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on developing Android applications for Android Automotive OS, covering testing, manifest configuration, and driver distraction requirements. The changes update the formatting of warnings and tips, add more emphasis on driver distraction, and clarify the use of CarUxRestrictionsManager for handling UX restrictions.

entries.title: Build browsers for Android Automotive OS
entries.summary: This document provides information on developing browser applications for Android Automotive OS. The changes improve formatting using markdown features like admonitions and adjust phrasing for clarity. The update emphasizes security requirements for handling sensitive user data, particularly in shared vehicle environments, and provides guidance on implementing device credential authentication.

entries.title: Build games for cars
entries.summary: The documentation for games on Android Auto and Android Automotive OS has been updated. The changes include rephrasing the introductory paragraph, updating the formatting for important and caution notes using markdown callouts, and refining the text for clarity. These changes provide more direct and clear information for developers integrating games for parked car experiences.

entries.title: Build video apps for Android Automotive OS
entries.summary: The documentation updates the video.md file. It converts the existing emphasis on design guidelines, and important notes into the NOTE and IMPORTANT blocks. It also updates the codelab link, and the caution sections into a caution block.

entries.title: Android Auto overview
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to use the 'Note' and 'Caution' blocks for emphasizing important information. It also refactored the formatting to use the standard markdown for Android documentation.

entries.title: Android Automotive OS overview
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to replace the use of the markdown bold syntax for notes and tips with the more structured admonition syntax using `> [!NOTE]` and `> [!TIP]`. This change improves the document's structure and potentially its rendering on different platforms.

entries.title: Coarse location on Android Automotive OS
entries.summary: The documentation for coarse location in Android Automotive OS has been updated. The primary change involves replacing the original note with a callout block, which indicates that automotive apps shouldn't rely on NLP or sensor fusion performed by the fused location provider (FLP).

entries.title: Android Automotive OS compatibility mode
entries.summary: This document describes Android Automotive OS compatibility mode, a software feature in certain cars to improve the user experience of mobile apps on Android Automotive OS. The changes include clarifications and reformatting of existing content, including more details regarding Back navigation, Safe area rendering, Density scaling, and Activity lifecycle. Additionally, the content was updated to include a caution about how this behavior applies to all apps running in compatibility mode, not just apps running in compatibility mode, and to update details around testing apps in compatibility mode.

entries.title: Google Play on Android Automotive OS
entries.summary: This document provides information about Google Play's functionality within Android Automotive OS. It details which Google Play services, libraries, and APIs are supported, as well as specific considerations for in-app purchases and licensing on automotive platforms. The changes mainly involve clarifying the capabilities and limitations of Google Play features in cars with Google built-in, and providing updated links and formatting.

entries.title: Google Play services for cars with Google built-in
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify that when developing apps for Android Automotive OS cars with Google built-in, certain Google Play services might be restricted to ensure a safe and consistent experience. It also emphasizes that developers can use the same client libraries for Google Play services as they do for mobile apps. The updated content now directly states that the provided table illustrates which Google APIs for Android are available on Android Automotive OS cars with Google built-in, and the table itself remains unchanged.

entries.title: Notifications on Android Automotive OS
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of how notifications function on Android Automotive OS. It details the differences in notification behavior compared to other Android devices, focusing on simplified user interactions, UX restrictions based on drive state, and specific requirements for messaging notifications to ensure driver safety and minimize distractions.

entries.title: Releases
entries.summary: This document details changes related to Android versions 12-16 that affect Android Auto and Android Automotive OS. It includes updates regarding window insets, foreground service types, runtime-registered broadcast receivers, activity launching, the locale of CarContext, UI mode, component exporting, and pending intent mutability to ensure app compatibility and correct behavior on different Android versions.

entries.title: Test Android apps for cars
entries.summary: This document introduces updates to the Android Auto and Android Automotive OS testing documentation. The changes include formatting adjustments, clarification of existing content, and minor wording improvements to enhance readability and provide better guidance to developers on testing their car applications.

entries.title: Test using Android Automotive OS on Pixel Tablet
entries.summary: This document has been updated to improve readability, clarity, and formatting.  Specifically, it uses callout boxes for notes, cautions, and important information.  It also updates links, and reformats the table in the "System images" section to improve readability.

entries.title: Test using the Desktop Head Unit
entries.summary: This document provides instructions for using the Desktop Head Unit (DHU) to test Android Auto apps. The changes include formatting updates, improved clarity with the use of callout boxes, and minor corrections to the instructions for installing and running the DHU.  The update clarifies the steps for connecting to the DHU using Accessory Mode and ADB Tunneling, which is important for developers testing Android Auto apps.

entries.title: Test using the configurable emulator
entries.summary: This document describes how to configure the Android Automotive emulator to mimic various hardware configurations seen in cars. The changes primarily refine the wording for clarity and consistency, and updates the code example. It also includes new recommendations for testing configurations, specifying combinations of hardware profiles and RROs.

entries.title: Test using the distant display emulator
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to improve readability and formatting. Changes include line breaks, clearer descriptions, and the use of a TIP block.

entries.title: Access Android Automotive OS devices through Firebase Test Lab
entries.summary: The document was updated to consolidate the text and formatting, and minor rewording. There were no meaningful changes to the technical content.

entries.title: Test interoperability with Google Services
entries.summary: The documentation for testing Google Assistant integration with Android Automotive has been updated. The changes include improvements in formatting and wording for clarity, as well as minor adjustments to the instructions for setting up the demo application and the inclusion of the required code snippets for handling intents.

entries.title: What's new
entries.summary: This document provides updates on the Android Auto and Android Automotive OS, including new codelabs, content updates and library releases.  Key changes include updates to existing codelabs, the addition of new sections on browser apps, media artwork, virtual displays, and custom metadata indicators, along with the availability of the AppSearch module and the addition of Android 16 information.

entries.title: Designing for foldables
entries.summary: This document describes features added to ConstraintLayout 2.1 to support foldable devices, including SharedValues, ReactiveGuide, and enhanced MotionLayout support for animations. These features enable developers to manage UI elements and animations based on the state of the foldable device, such as the fold position or device orientation.

entries.title: MotionLayout reference
entries.summary: The document introduces MotionLayout and motion scenes. It clarifies that a motion scene file is an XML file that defines a motion sequence, referenced by the `app:layoutDescription` attribute in the layout file's `<MotionLayout>` node. This overview helps developers understand the core concepts and setup for using MotionLayout.

The documentation for `<Constraint>` element in MotionLayout has been updated. A video link has been added, and the documentation now explicitly states that the `<Constraint>` element supports standard ConstraintLayout attributes, directing users to the ConstraintLayout developer guide for details. This update provides direct access to video tutorials and clarifies the element's behavior.

The documentation for `ConstraintSet` has been updated with a link to a YouTube video and some minor formatting changes for clarity. Specifically, a video link was added and some lines were reformatted for better readability.

The documentation for `<KeyFrameSet>` has been updated to include a link to a video explaining how to use it. It also refines existing explanations for clarity and readability, clarifying how `<KeyFrameSet>` modifies the behavior of MotionLayout animations by using KeyPosition and KeyAttribute to specify intermediate states.

The documentation for `<KeyPosition>` has been updated to include a link to a video tutorial, improve the explanation of the element's purpose, and refine the descriptions of attributes. These changes enhance clarity and provide users with a better understanding of how to use `<KeyPosition>` to customize motion paths in MotionLayout.
The documentation for `motionscene.md` has been updated with minor formatting changes, clarifying language, and improved syntax. A video link has also been added at the beginning of the file.
The documentation for `<OnClick>` in MotionLayout has been updated. A video link has been added, and minor text refinements improve clarity, such as breaking up long sentences for better readability.

The documentation for `OnSwipe` has been updated with minor formatting improvements, and a link to a video tutorial has been added. These changes enhance readability and provide additional learning resources for developers.

The documentation for `<Transition>` in MotionLayout has been updated. A video link has been added, and minor adjustments were made to the text, including formatting and wording improvements for better clarity. The changes clarify how to specify start and end states within the transition definition.

entries.title: Data and file storage overview
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to use the `[!CAUTION]` and `[!NOTE]` callout blocks instead of the older `| **Caution:**` and `| **Note:**` formatting. This improves the visual presentation and consistency of the documentation.

entries.title: Access app-specific files
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to reflect UI changes. It replaces the old "Note" and "Caution" callouts with new "NOTE" and "CAUTION" UI elements to improve readability. There were also minor wording adjustments for clarity.

entries.title: Save data in a local database using Room
entries.summary: The documentation for Room has been updated to improve readability and clarity. Changes include better formatting, improved explanations, and code examples. Some sections were also updated to improve clarity, such as the note about choosing between `ksp` and `annotationProcessor` in the build.gradle file. Additional formatting changes were done to improve the appearance, and some minor wording changes were made.

entries.title: Accessing data using Room DAOs
entries.summary: This document introduces Data Access Objects (DAOs) in Room, explaining their role in interacting with a database. It covers the anatomy of a DAO, including convenience methods for basic operations (insert, update, delete) and query methods for more complex interactions, with examples in Kotlin and Java.

entries.title: Write asynchronous DAO queries
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to modernize the formatting with the use of markdown callouts instead of the deprecated bolded "Note:" for important information and to update the layout for the table. These changes improve readability and maintain consistency with current documentation practices.

entries.title: Create views into a database
entries.summary: The documentation introduces and explains the usage of SQLite database views within the Room persistence library, starting from version 2.1.0. It details how to create views using the `@DatabaseView` annotation, including an example, and how to associate them with the database through the `@Database` annotation's `views` property. These views allow encapsulating queries and are treated similarly to data objects within DAOs, enabling complex data retrieval operations.

entries.title: Define data using Room entities
entries.summary: The documentation for defining Room entities has been updated to clarify concepts, improve clarity, and provide more detailed explanations of features like FTS and AutoValue integration. The changes include reformatting, enhanced notes, and more specific examples.

entries.title: Migrate your Room database
entries.summary: This document describes how to migrate Room database versions. It explains automatic and manual migration strategies, the use of `AutoMigrationSpec` for complex schema changes in automated migrations, and the process of testing migrations using the `room-testing` Maven artifact, including schema export and configuration options for different Room versions and build variants.

entries.title: Prepopulate your Room database
entries.summary: This document introduces prepopulating a Room database, explaining how to initialize a Room database with data from a prepackaged database file. It covers prepopulation from assets and the file system, along with how it affects database migrations.

entries.title: Referencing complex data using Room
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify the use of type converters, including how to handle their initialization and scoping. It also reinforces the reason why Room doesn't allow object references, explaining the performance implications of lazy loading on the UI thread and suggesting the use of POJOs and joins for referencing multiple entities.

entries.title: Choose relationship types between objects
entries.summary: This document introduces improvements in formatting and wording for the Room relationships documentation. These changes enhance readability and clarity, including the use of markdown for better formatting and minor adjustments to existing content.

entries.title: Define and query nested relationships
entries.summary: The documentation for nested relationships in Room has been updated to clarify the concept and improve readability. The changes include rephrasing the introductory paragraph, converting the caution box to the new alert style, and refining the wording of several sentences. These changes aim to enhance the clarity and understanding of how to implement nested relationships within Room, including best practices for performance considerations. There are no code changes, only wording.

entries.title: Define and query one-to-many relationships
entries.summary: This document explains how to define and query one-to-many relationships in a Room database. It describes the necessary steps: defining entities, specifying relationships with annotations like `@Relation`, and querying data using a DAO with `@Transaction`.

entries.title: Define and query one-to-one relationships
entries.summary: The documentation for one-to-one relationships in Room was updated to correct a minor grammatical error and improve clarity in the descriptions of the `@Relation` annotation and the use of the `@Transaction` annotation. These changes enhance the readability of the documentation for developers learning to implement one-to-one relationships in their Android applications.

entries.title: Migrate from SQLite to Room
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on migrating from a non-Room SQLite implementation to Room. It outlines the steps involved, including updating dependencies, model classes, DAOs, and database instantiation. The document also provides a guide to incremental migration and links to additional resources.

entries.title: Test and debug your database
entries.summary: This document underwent several changes, mainly focusing on improving readability and clarity. These changes include rewriting sentences, replacing markdown syntax, and adding some code snippets. The core content and technical information remain unchanged.

entries.title: Save simple data with SharedPreferences
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to include more emphasis on using DataStore instead of SharedPreferences and improved the formatting of the existing documentation. Also, the documentation has been updated to use the latest markdown format.

entries.title: Overview of shared storage
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify the purpose of shared storage as a place for user data accessible to other apps and preserved even after app uninstallation.  It also slightly changed the wording and formatting of the listed shared data types such as Media content, Documents and other files, and Datasets.

entries.title: Dependency injection in Android
entries.summary: This document provides a comprehensive overview of dependency injection (DI) in Android development. It explains the fundamentals of DI, its advantages, different approaches like constructor and field injection, and the benefits of automated DI using libraries like Dagger. It also contrasts DI with the service locator pattern.

entries.title: Using Dagger in Android apps
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to reflect best practices and clarifies how to use Dagger in Android applications, including field injection, activity injection, and module definitions, along with code examples. It also recommends using Hilt for dependency injection.

entries.title: Dagger basics
entries.summary: This document introduces Dagger, a dependency injection framework, and explains its benefits over manual dependency injection. It describes how Dagger generates code at compile time, creating factories and dependency graphs, and how to use scopes to manage object lifecycles. It also shows a simple use case of generating a factory and how to use Dagger components for dependency injection.

entries.title: Using Dagger in multi-module apps
entries.summary: This document introduces component dependencies in Dagger for multi-module Android projects, especially when using feature modules where dependencies are inverted. It explains how to use component dependencies instead of subcomponents to resolve dependency issues between the app and feature modules, including code examples for implementation. Key changes include updated code examples and clarifications on how component dependencies work in contrast to subcomponents.

entries.title: Hilt and Dagger annotations cheat sheet
entries.summary: The Hilt cheat sheet documentation was updated. The changes include formatting adjustments for better readability, such as italicizing the words 'what' and 'how'.

entries.title: Use Hilt with other Jetpack libraries
entries.summary: The documentation for Hilt-Jetpack has been updated to improve clarity and formatting. Changes include minor text adjustments, improved code formatting, and the addition of callouts for notes and warnings. These updates enhance readability and provide better guidance for developers using Hilt with Jetpack libraries.

entries.title: Hilt in multi-module apps
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify how to use Hilt and Dagger in multi-module Android projects, especially those with feature modules.  It refines the explanation of using component dependencies for feature modules and provides more context and links to relevant documentation.

entries.title: Manual dependency injection
entries.summary: The documentation for manual dependency injection has been updated with formatting changes, more detailed explanations, and code examples. Specifically, it includes improved explanations of how to manage dependencies in application flows, addressing the challenges of scoping objects to specific flows and the creation/removal of flow containers.

entries.title: Support different pixel densities
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to include a video overview and uses direct links to the relevant Android developer documentation for classes like `TypedValue` and `ViewConfiguration` instead of the short-hand style. It also updates the links for `Bitmap`.

entries.title: Declare package visibility needs
entries.summary: The documentation introduces changes related to the formatting of notes, updating the syntax to use the newer `> [!NOTE]` Markdown syntax. This improves the visual presentation and consistency of notes within the documentation.

entries.title: Declare app permissions
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify the distinction between install-time, runtime, and special permissions and how they are handled.  It also updates the code examples and improves the formatting of cautions and warnings.

entries.title: Request special permissions
entries.summary: The documentation for requesting special permissions has been updated to include a visual aid of the 'Special app access' screen in system settings, and the formatting of the note has been enhanced for clarity. Also, there are minor adjustments to wording and formatting throughout the document. These changes clarify how to grant special permissions and emphasize the importance of using them judiciously.

entries.title: Request runtime permissions
entries.summary: The changes update code examples related to permission requests in Android apps. Specifically, it changes `showInContext>UI(...)` to `showInContextUI(...)` and also changes `else - {` to `else -> {` in the Kotlin code examples. This adjustment likely aims to fix code formatting and improve readability within the context of permission handling.

entries.title: Restrict interactions with other apps
entries.summary: This document was updated to use more modern markdown formatting, like the use of the note boxes. The document also had its wording improved and links updated.

entries.title: Test apps on Android
entries.summary: The documentation for testing Android apps has been updated to include the Android Jetpack link. This change adds context to the document and relates the testing topic to a broader Android ecosystem, which helps users understand the framework and resources.

entries.title: Continuous Integration basics
entries.summary: The document introduces Continuous Integration (CI) and its benefits for Android development. It explains how CI systems work, including a typical workflow, and highlights the advantages of using CI, such as early bug detection and increased code quality. The changes include rephrasing for clarity, improved formatting, and minor wording adjustments.

entries.title: Types of CI automation
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of CI automation for Android development. It covers basic jobs like builds and lint checks, instrumented tests using emulators and device farms, performance regression tests using benchmark libraries, and test coverage regression checks. The changes include slight wording adjustments and improved formatting for better readability, and the addition of a note about different coverage metrics.

entries.title: CI features
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to include formatting and wording improvements throughout the document. Also, it updates the note about build cache and add details on the Gradle's build cache feature.

entries.title: Espresso
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of Espresso, an Android UI testing framework. The changes include improvements to formatting, clarifying synchronization conditions, and refining descriptions of various Espresso packages and resources. These changes enhance the clarity and readability of the documentation for developers using Espresso.

entries.title: Accessibility checking
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify and improve the readability of existing content.  It also refactors existing sentences for conciseness and clarity, and to use more current links.

entries.title: Additional Resources for Espresso
entries.summary: This document provides a list of sample projects demonstrating various Espresso testing techniques. The changes include slight formatting adjustments and Markdown syntax updates for consistency and readability.

entries.title: Espresso basics
entries.summary: The documentation for Espresso basics has been updated to improve clarity and style. Specifically, the changes include minor wording adjustments, improved formatting with markdown, and the addition of callout boxes using the "NOTE" syntax to highlight important information. These changes make the documentation more readable and easier to understand, improving the overall learning experience for developers using Espresso for testing Android applications.

entries.title: Espresso cheat sheet
entries.summary: The cheat sheet was updated with a clearer introductory statement, and the file now includes an image that visually represents key Espresso methods, enhancing the guide's utility for developers. This provides a more immediate and accessible reference for Espresso testing.

entries.title: Espresso idling resources
entries.summary: The documentation for Espresso idling resources was updated. Hyperlinks to Java classes were updated to use the full URL, and notes were formatted using the callout block syntax.

entries.title: Espresso lists
entries.summary: This document provides updated guidance on testing lists using Espresso, including how to interact with `AdapterView` and `RecyclerView` objects. It clarifies the use of `onData()` for `AdapterView` and `RecyclerViewActions` for `RecyclerView` and includes examples and links to relevant code samples.

entries.title: Multiprocess Espresso
entries.summary: This document introduces Multiprocess Espresso, a tool for testing UI interactions across different processes within an Android app, available from Android 8.0 (API level 26) and higher. It explains how to set up the testing environment, including adding dependencies in the `build.gradle` file and configuring the `AndroidManifest.xml` file, and provides an overview of the tool's architecture. Several minor wording, formatting, and link updates are included.

entries.title: Espresso recipes
entries.summary: This document provides examples and code snippets for common Espresso test scenarios, including matching views based on siblings, action bar items, and data within adapters, along with assertions for view presence and custom failure handling. It also covers targeting non-default windows and matching header/footer elements in list views. These changes provide more complete examples of how to write various Espresso tests.

entries.title: Fundamentals of testing Android apps
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of testing Android applications, detailing the benefits, types of tests (functional, performance), and the distinction between instrumented and local tests. It also covers testable architectures and decoupling techniques to facilitate effective testing.

entries.title: Testing strategies
entries.summary: This document introduces and explains strategies for Android app testing. It has been updated to include a video link, clarify existing explanations of testing categories, and refine the descriptions of each test type (unit, component, feature, application, and release candidate).

entries.title: Use test doubles in Android
entries.summary: The documentation on test doubles in Android has been updated with minor text adjustments. It clarifies the context of test doubles, elaborates on hermetic testing, and provides a reference to Hilt for dependency injection to facilitate testing.  The document also has additional notes, warnings, and formatting changes.

entries.title: What to test in Android
entries.summary: The documentation on what to test in Android applications was updated to improve clarity and formatting. Changes include adjustments to wording, organization, and the addition of a note about test coverage. The content retains the core principles of Android testing practices.

entries.title: Build instrumented tests
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated with more clear and concise descriptions.  The changes include improved formatting, clearer explanations of instrumented tests and their setup, and use of the new AndroidX Test libraries.  These changes provide more guidance on how to write instrumented tests effectively, and improve the readability of the document.

entries.title: JUnit4 rules with AndroidX Test
entries.summary: The documentation for AndroidX Test's JUnit rules has been updated. Formatting changes include adjustments to spacing, improved code block presentation, and the addition of a note about fragment testing using `FragmentScenario` and `IntentService` limitations. These changes improve readability and clarity for developers using these testing tools.

entries.title: AndroidJUnitRunner
entries.summary: The documentation for AndroidJUnitRunner has been updated with improved formatting and minor content revisions.  The changes include clarifying the use of the runner, updating code snippets for clarity, and expanding explanations of features like the Android Test Orchestrator, including its benefits and enabling steps.

entries.title: Set up project for AndroidX Test
entries.summary: The documentation for setting up AndroidX Test libraries has been updated. The changes include improvements in formatting and the addition of callout boxes with 'Note', 'Warning', and 'Caution' to highlight important information. Additionally, some text has been reworded for clarity.

entries.title: Performance tests
entries.summary: The documentation on performance testing has been updated to improve readability and clarity. These changes include line breaks for better formatting and minor wording adjustments throughout the document. Also, the text has been formatted to use more precise language and consistency.

entries.title: Big test stability
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of instrumented test stability in Android, emphasizing how to write reliable and repeatable tests by addressing asynchronous behavior and synchronization issues. It includes information on test synchronization, ways to improve stability, configuring devices, preventing synchronization issues, and retry mechanisms. The document has been updated with more details about synchronization, use of coroutines and RxJava for testing, and more recommendations on how to deal with flaky tests.

entries.title: Build local unit tests
entries.summary: This document introduces local unit testing in Android. It explains how to set up local tests, add dependencies, and write test classes. The changes include formatting updates, clarification of concepts like test doubles and mocks, and revised example code snippets. The document also provides a guide on how to mock Android dependencies using the Mockable Android library and Mockito, along with a discussion on how to handle the "Method ... not mocked" error and the use of 'returnDefaultValues'.

entries.title: Robolectric strategies
entries.summary: This document introduces Robolectric, an open-source framework for testing Android applications. The document provides an overview of Robolectric, highlighting its benefits, limitations, and use cases for unit and UI testing. It explains the framework's advantages in terms of speed and reliability while running tests in a simulated Android environment, and also offers recommendations and best practices for its use.

entries.title: Test content providers
entries.summary: The documentation for testing content providers has been updated to include a note about `ProviderTestRule` replacing `ProviderTestCase2`. The document also includes formatting and minor wording improvements throughout the document, enhancing clarity and readability.

entries.title: Test your service
entries.summary: The documentation for testing Android services has been updated to clarify the use of `ServiceTestRule` for testing local services. It also provides clearer instructions on setting up the testing environment and creating integration tests, including links to relevant documentation.

entries.title: Use the UI Automator legacy API
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to fix several minor issues. The changes include correcting a typo in a code example, updating a text comparison, and resolving minor formatting issues to improve readability and code correctness.

entries.title: Automate UI tests
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated with minor formatting changes and some rewording for improved clarity. These changes do not affect the overall technical content of the document.

entries.title: Behavior UI Tests
entries.summary: The documentation for Behavior UI tests was updated. Specifically, there were minor formatting adjustments, including the addition of blank lines for improved readability and the correction of minor inconsistencies in text formatting.

entries.title: Screenshot testing
entries.summary: This document on screenshot testing has been updated to improve clarity and formatting. Changes include the addition of callout boxes for important information, such as warnings and key points. Additionally, minor rewording and formatting changes were made throughout the document to enhance readability. The image captions were also updated.

entries.title: Android TV overview
entries.summary: The documentation introduces a video link at the beginning. The content also includes minor formatting and wording adjustments in the initial paragraph.

entries.title: Custom view accessibility sample for Android TV
entries.summary: The changes primarily involve correcting and clarifying code within the `SampleExploreByTouchHelper` class. Specifically, the changes fix incorrect syntax in the Kotlin and Java versions of the code, and improve readability. These fixes ensure proper functionality of accessibility features such as Explore by Touch.

entries.title: Custom view accessibility support on Android TV
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on implementing accessibility support for custom views in Android TV applications. It emphasizes the importance of providing accessibility services with information about custom views, especially when dealing with OpenGL or Canvas, to ensure proper function with Talkback and Switch Access. The document offers solutions and best practices to address common accessibility issues.

entries.title: Adopt system caption settings
entries.summary: This guide now clarifies that it demonstrates how an app can obtain and apply the system-provided caption style on Android TV. The introductory text and some phrasing were updated for clarity, and minor formatting changes were made. The guide continues to explain how to get the CaptioningManager and handle caption style changes.

entries.title: Support TalkBack in TV apps
entries.summary: The documentation for supporting TalkBack in TV apps has been updated. The changes focus on improving clarity and formatting, while providing more detail on how to make views focusable, modify action descriptions, and implement slider support.

entries.title: TalkBack evaluation examples for TV apps
entries.summary: The document has been updated to improve readability and clarity. Changes include formatting adjustments, minor wording refinements, and consistent use of bold formatting. These changes improve the guidance on evaluating TV apps for TalkBack accessibility, such as adding more instructions on how to evaluate the app, as well as fixing a minor formatting issue.

entries.title: Help users find your content on Android TV
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of how users can discover content on Android TV. The changes primarily involve minor text adjustments, such as improved formatting, HTML to Markdown conversion, and updated links to related documentation, but the core concepts regarding content recommendation and searchability remain the same.

entries.title: Audio program attributes
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to improve readability and formatting of the table describing audio program attributes.  The table's markdown was also corrected.

entries.title: Game program attributes
entries.summary: The documentation for game program attributes has been updated to improve readability and formatting.  The changes include minor text revisions and a table reformatting for better presentation.

entries.title: Watch Next guidelines for app developers
entries.summary: The documentation for Android TV's Watch Next channel guidelines has been updated with minor clarifications and formatting adjustments.  Key changes include specifying that content should be added to the Watch Next channel immediately when the user exits the app or pauses playback for more than 5 minutes, and that the Content ID attribute must be set for every video program to match the internal ID in the Media Actions feed.

entries.title: Watch Next guidelines for TV providers
entries.summary: This document provides guidelines for TV providers regarding the Watch Next channel. The changes enhance clarity and formatting, including improvements to the structure, formatting, and wording, such as the addition of markdown formatting for clarity, and improved sentence structure for better readability. These changes ensure the guidelines are clear and easy to understand for developers, leading to a better user experience on Android TV.

entries.title: Preview videos
entries.summary: This document describes the process of creating and rendering preview videos for Android TV applications. The changes include improvements in formatting, links to references, and clarifications of existing content. These updates provide better clarity and a more user-friendly experience for developers creating preview videos.

entries.title: Channels on the home screen
entries.summary: This document introduces how to add and manage recommendation channels and programs on the Android TV home screen. The changes include improvements in formatting, updated links, and clarification of existing concepts. Several code examples were also updated.

entries.title: Recommend content on the home screen
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify how Android TV displays recommendations using channels and programs. It also provides more detail on the default channel and the Watch Next channel, and compatibility considerations for different Android versions, including a note about the temporary nature of forward-compatible behavior.

entries.title: Video program attributes
entries.summary: The table displaying video program attributes has been reformatted for improved readability. The markdown table format was adjusted to improve clarity, with no functional changes to the content.

entries.title: Add programs to the Watch Next channel
entries.summary: The documentation for adding programs to the Watch Next channel has been updated. The changes include more descriptive text about the channel, including details about the types of programs that can be added and the process for certification on Google TV. The formatting has also been slightly updated for better readability.

entries.title: Watch Next attributes
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to reflect improvements in formatting and clarity. The text was updated to use a note block and the table's formatting was updated.

entries.title: Build TV games
entries.summary: The documentation for Android TV games has been updated to provide more accurate links for Android developers. It updates hyperlinks for methods and attributes, and fixes minor formatting issues.

entries.title: Best practices to drive engagement on Google TV
entries.summary: This document provides best practices for developing apps for Google TV, covering key areas like content discovery, voice integration, and user acquisition.  It suggests that developers support Google Cast, media sessions, media actions feeds, Watch Next, account linking, entitlement sync, voice casting, Cast Connect, Google Play Billing, and frictionless subscriptions to improve the user experience on Google TV. Also added formatting and markdown improvements.

entries.title: AndroidX TV libraries
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to emphasize the use of Android architecture components like ViewModel and Room, along with Compose, to share application logic between form factors when building Android TV apps. This promotes modern UI development and efficient code reuse.

entries.title: Multitasking on TV
entries.summary: The documentation updates incorporate changes to formatting, and use of `[!NOTE]` for notes. Additionally, there are minor adjustments to code examples and clarifications regarding the `preferKeepClear` attribute and the usage of picture-in-picture mode. These changes improve readability and clarity.

entries.title: TV navigation
entries.summary: This document provides an overview of navigation on Android TV devices. The changes include formatting improvements, updates to the wording, and minor clarifications to the existing content. The document also provides a better note about the remote controller and its features.

entries.title: On-screen keyboard
entries.summary: The documentation for on-screen keyboards on Android TV has been updated to reflect improvements and features in Gboard. Key changes include enhanced formatting for readability, updated descriptions, and clarified information about keyboard placement and alignment options. This ensures developers can easily implement and customize on-screen keyboards for their Android TV applications.

entries.title: Media Quality framework
entries.summary: The documentation for the `PictureProfile` object was updated. No substantive changes were made; the content was reworded for clarity.

entries.title: Ambient Mode
entries.summary: The documentation for Android TV's Ambient Mode has been updated. The changes include text formatting, the addition of a note block, and minor wording adjustments for clarity.

entries.title: Leanback UI toolkit libraries
entries.summary: The documentation updates reflect that the Leanback library is deprecated, with instructions to use Jetpack Compose for Android TV OS. Additionally, some minor formatting changes and clarifications are made throughout the document. It also includes an update on the limitation of the Leanback tab library and its theme supports.

entries.title: Migrate to Compose for TV
entries.summary: The documentation provides updated guidance on migrating from Leanback UI toolkit to Compose for Android TV. It emphasizes a gradual migration approach, leveraging dedicated Compose components, adapting data and state management, and testing throughout the process. The changes include improved formatting and clearer wording for better understanding.

entries.title: Use transport controls
entries.summary: This document has been significantly updated to reflect the deprecation of the Leanback library and the recommendation to use Jetpack Compose for Android TV OS instead. It also incorporates minor updates to improve clarity and formatting, including the use of more concise descriptions and updated links.

entries.title: Implement a media session
entries.summary: The documentation for media sessions has been updated to improve readability and clarity. The changes include formatting adjustments, such as adding line breaks and clarifying sentences for better understanding.

entries.title: Optimize memory usage
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on optimizing memory usage for Android TV apps, focusing on the specific challenges and constraints of TV devices, particularly those with low RAM. It highlights the potential problems caused by high memory consumption and defines memory targets for different device types.

entries.title: Apps
entries.summary: The documentation updates provide clearer explanations and improved formatting for app development on Wear OS. The changes enhance readability and clarify important concepts such as the use of Jetpack Compose and UX principles.

entries.title: Appear in Recents and app resume
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to improve clarity and formatting. Changes include minor rewording and improved formatting in the text, and improved code samples.

entries.title: Conserve power and battery
entries.summary: This document focuses on improving power efficiency in Wear OS apps. It emphasizes the importance of minimizing battery drain due to the smaller battery size of Wear OS devices and provides best practices to achieve this, including deferring tasks, utilizing always-on mode, and minimizing screen-on time and CPU usage.

entries.title: Test Bluetooth audio on emulators
entries.summary: This document explains how to test Bluetooth audio functionality on Wear OS emulators. It guides users on pairing with emulated phones and playing audio through emulated Bluetooth output devices, providing steps for setting up the environment using the Bumble project.

entries.title: Migrate from Material 2.5 to Material 3 in Compose for Wear OS
entries.summary: This guide provides instructions and considerations for migrating Android Wear OS applications built with Wear Compose Material 2.5 to the new Wear Compose Material 3 Jetpack library. The changes include adding a description, keywords, and updating a link to a snippet file in the navigation example.

entries.title: Discover devices on a network using Data Layer APIs
entries.summary: This document describes how to discover devices on a network using Data Layer APIs. The changes include improvements to the readability of the text, and improved formatting.

entries.title: Connect a watch to a phone
entries.summary: This document provides updated instructions and clarifies the process of connecting a Wear OS watch to a phone, focusing on emulator pairing and phone setup. It includes updated steps and UI references for using the Wear OS emulator pairing assistant and setting up a phone to connect with a Wear OS watch, and addresses details about pairing multiple devices.

entries.title: Create and run your first Wear OS app
entries.summary: This document provides a guide for creating a Wear OS app using a template in Android Studio. It also introduces best practices for Wear OS app development. The changes include improvements in text formatting, rewording, and updates to the links and notes.

entries.title: Debug a Wear OS app
entries.summary: This document provides instructions for debugging Wear OS applications. It outlines the debugging process using both the Android Emulator and physical devices, including setup and connection methods. The document also includes information on installing specific OS versions, testing watch-phone connections, and capturing screenshots and videos.

entries.title: Test on the Wear OS emulator
entries.summary: This document provides guidance on testing Wear OS applications using the Android Emulator, highlighting specialized features, testing capabilities, and troubleshooting tips specific to Wear OS development. It covers sensor simulation, input simulation, device pairing, and known issues.

entries.title: Capture Wear UI screens
entries.summary: The documentation for capturing Wear OS UI screens has been updated. Formatting, link, and wording improvements were made for clarity and consistency. Minor clarifications were added for using adb screencap and scrcpy for screen recordings, and companion app screenshots.

entries.title: Develop experiences for kids on Wear OS
entries.summary: This document provides guidelines for developing Wear OS apps and watch faces optimized for kids, emphasizing battery life, standalone app considerations, and the use of Watch Face Format. The changes include improved formatting, clarification of standalone mode behavior, and the introduction of a method for displaying legal information using short-links or QR codes when the device is in standalone mode. It also clarifies best practices for checking the standalone mode status before authorization requests.

entries.title: Principles of Wear OS development
entries.summary: This document provides principles for developing Wear OS applications. The changes include improvements in the text, formatting, and some additions. The content emphasizes designing for core tasks, optimizing for the wrist, and respecting user privacy, providing more detailed guidance on how to implement each principle.

entries.title: Tiles
entries.summary: The documentation has undergone minor formatting changes, including line breaks and spacing adjustments. Additionally, link formatting was updated. These changes do not introduce new technical information.

entries.title: Tile animations
entries.summary: The documentation for animations in Wear tiles was updated. Specifically, the Kotlin code examples for animations, slide transitions, and transformations were updated and the data type for onTileRequest and onTileResourcesRequest has been updated.

entries.title: Lifecycle and analytics events
entries.summary: The documentation for Wear OS Tiles lifecycle was updated. Specifically, the text 'Caution:' was updated to use a callout block for emphasis.

entries.title: Wear OS user interfaces
entries.summary: The documentation updates include cosmetic changes to improve readability, such as replacing markdown syntax and refining wording.  The note regarding Compose for Wear OS has been updated to use the appropriate markdown syntax, and image spacing has been fixed.

entries.title: Prepare your app for behavior changes in Wear OS 4
entries.summary: This document outlines changes in Wear OS 4, which is based on Android 13 (API level 33). It emphasizes the need to handle system behavior changes from Android 12 and 13. The document also provides information on how to improve app compatibility by targeting Android 13.

entries.title: Prepare your app for behavior changes in Wear OS 4
entries.summary: Wear OS 4 introduces new permission requirements for apps. Notably, apps now require notification runtime permission by default and background body sensors permission. Apps should also account for approximate location permission. This affects how apps request and handle user permissions for notifications, background sensor access, and location data.

entries.title: Prepare your app for behavior changes in Wear OS 4
entries.summary: Significant changes to app components and navigation are detailed, including stricter intent filter matching, modified root launcher activity behavior, stricter Android App Links verification, and the removal of the SYSTEM_ALERT_WINDOW UI. These changes impact how apps handle intents, lifecycle management, deep linking, and UI display.

entries.title: Prepare your app for behavior changes in Wear OS 4
entries.summary: This section covers modifications to power and data management, including the Restricted App Standby Bucket, App Hibernation, and Backup & Restore functionality. These changes impact app behavior in low-usage scenarios and introduce cloud backup capabilities.

entries.title: Explore features in Wear OS 4
entries.summary: This document outlines the new features introduced in Wear OS 4, including enhancements to watch faces, tiles, splash screens, and data transfer to new mobile devices. These updates provide developers with new tools and improve the user experience on Wear OS devices.

entries.title: Wear OS 5.1
entries.summary: This document introduces Wear OS 5.1, based on Android 15 (API level 35), and guides developers on preparing devices and emulators for testing, updating the target SDK, and exploring new features such as Credential Manager support and watch speaker playback.

entries.title: Test how your app handles behavior changes
entries.summary: This document outlines the changes and behavior changes in Wear OS 5, which is based on Android 14. It highlights changes affecting all apps and those specifically for apps targeting Android 14, including privacy dashboard support, watch face format requirements, backgrounding of always-on apps, foreground service type declarations for exercise-recording apps, device unlock duration, handling of draggable content, restrictions to implicit intents, and non-dismissible notifications.

entries.title: Explore features
entries.summary: This document outlines new features in Wear OS 5, including a grid-based app launcher, user control over media output devices, enhancements to Watch Face Format (including flavors, new complication types, weather data, and heart rate data), screenshot detection API, and updates to Health Services (detailed running metrics and debounced goals). These features improve the user experience and offer developers more tools for creating engaging and informative Wear OS apps.

entries.title: Update your app's target SDK version for Wear OS 5
entries.summary: This document provides instructions on how to update your Wear OS app to target Android 14 (API level 34). It guides developers through modifying the build.gradle or build.gradle.kts file to specify the target SDK version, highlighting the importance of handling system behavior changes for apps targeting Android 14 or higher.

entries.title: Test how your app handles behavior changes
entries.summary: This document outlines changes for Wear OS 6, based on Android 16 (API level 36). Key changes include a more consistent always-on display, default system font for tiles, more granular health permissions, and batched tile interaction events. These changes affect app behavior and require developers to update their apps for compatibility.

entries.title: Explore features
entries.summary: This document introduces the features of Wear OS 6, including support for Material 3 Expressive and enhancements to watch faces, along with continued support for features from previous versions. The update provides links to resources for migration and design principles, and it highlights improvements in layout and user experience, like expressive motion and multi-slot tile layouts, along with features in Watch Face Format v4 such as photo support and animated transitions.

entries.title: Prepare for Wear OS 6
entries.summary: This document provides instructions for setting up and testing Wear OS 6 emulators, and updating apps to target Wear OS 6. It includes updated instructions and notes on emulator setup, including downloading the latest Android Studio preview, and selecting the correct system image. It also adds a note about app publishing requirements on Google Play.

entries.title: Handle different watch shapes
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to reflect the current best practices for Wear OS development. It promotes the use of Jetpack Compose for UI development, provides updated links to the Wear OS UI Library and design guidelines, and clarifies the usage of BoxInsetLayout and WearableRecyclerView for supporting different watch shapes.  The code examples are updated with correct links and formatting.

entries.title: Customize watch face appearance using Watch Face Designer
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to rephrase the introduction and convert the existing note format to a more modern "NOTE" block. These changes improve readability and clarity.

entries.title: Advanced usage of Watch Face Designer
entries.summary: The documentation for advanced watch face design in Figma was updated to use the standard note block formatting for the note about folder representation in styles, changing the markdown syntax from a pipe-based table to a note block.

entries.title: Basics of using Watch Face Designer
entries.summary: The documentation for watch face designers was updated to replace a standard markdown note with an admonition block using the `> [!NOTE]` syntax. This change highlights the platform dependency of the user-provided photo feature, clarifying that it's only available on Wear OS 6 or higher.

entries.title: Export your watch face
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to use the `> [!NOTE]` callout syntax instead of bolded "Note:" text to highlight important information. This change improves the visual clarity and consistency of the documentation.

entries.title: Copyright (c) 2015 Calvin Metcalf
entries.summary: This document, which lists the open-source licenses used in the Watch Face Designer, has been updated. The changes primarily involve formatting improvements to the license text, like line breaks and spacing, to enhance readability. There are no substantial content changes.

entries.title: Publish from Watch Face Designer to Google Play
entries.summary: The documentation for publishing watch faces created with Watch Face Designer has been updated to use the modern "NOTE" and "CAUTION" callout blocks instead of bolded text for emphasis, and updated the images in the doc to reflect the latest UI changes. This improves readability and provides a more consistent look and feel with other Android documentation.

entries.title: Configure your phone app for Watch Face Push
entries.summary: This document introduces changes to the Watch Face Push library, focusing on providing guidance for developers on setting a watch face and handling different scenarios during the activation process. It also clarifies the use of the library and suggests alternatives like Watch Face Designer or Watch Face Studio if publishing to Google Play.

entries.title: Add complications to a watch face
entries.summary: This document introduces how to integrate and set up watch face complications on Wear OS utilizing the Watch Face Format. It outlines the available complication types and their fields, providing guidance on displaying data from various sources.

entries.title: Improve your watch face performance with hardware acceleration
entries.summary: The documentation for hardware acceleration in Wear OS watch faces was updated to remove a redundant introductory sentence and to correct a minor formatting error in the ADB command example by removing unnecessary backticks.

entries.title: Watch Face Format
entries.summary: The documentation for Wear OS watch face creation has been updated. The changes include an update to a help center article link and a formatting change for the "Note" section, using the `> [!NOTE]` syntax to emphasize it. This change provides enhanced clarity to the reader.

entries.title: Build and deploy a watch face
entries.summary: The documentation for building Wear OS watch faces using the Watch Face Format has been updated. The changes include improved formatting, clearer descriptions of the watch face structure, and better explanations of the build process using Android Studio and Gradle. Additionally, the documentation now emphasizes pre-submission checks for watch faces before submitting to the Google Play Store, including details on the Memory Footprint Evaluator tool.

entries.title: Debug a watch face
entries.summary: This document describes how to debug a watch face built with the Watch Face Format. The primary change is reformatting and minor clarifications regarding XML validation and runtime error identification using the XML validation tool and logcat, respectively. It explains how to check for valid Watch Face Format documents and how to identify runtime errors.

entries.title: Support personalization
entries.summary: The documentation introduces the concept of watch face personalization, explaining how to allow users to customize both the watch face's appearance and the information it displays. It highlights the use of `UserConfigurations` for appearance customization and Complications for information customization. It also emphasizes the role of the Watch Face Editor for user changes and the need to set the watch face as editable to enable the editor.

entries.title: Work profile contacts
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to include better formatting, more details on UX, and clarify IT admin behavior. It also includes examples and clarifications for enterprise contacts.

entries.title: Build a device policy controller
entries.summary: This document introduces changes related to the deprecation of custom DPC registration, and updates the documentation to reflect the latest guidance. It adds a caution about the deprecation of custom DPCs and provides updated links and formatting.

entries.title: Dedicated devices overview
entries.summary: The documentation introduces APIs for managing Android devices dedicated to a specific purpose. This guide is aimed at enterprise mobility management (EMM) developers and solution integrators.

entries.title: Dedicated devices cookbook
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to improve clarity and formatting. Changes include adjustments to the wording and code examples for better readability and a more consistent presentation.  These changes enhance the user experience by making the content easier to understand and follow.

entries.title: Lock task mode
entries.summary: This document introduces minor formatting and wording changes. It adds a more prominent 'Important' note to the 'Key Term' section. It also adds table formatting using markdown syntax and refactors various code snippets.

entries.title: Device control
entries.summary: The documentation was updated with minor formatting changes, link updates, and clarifications. The most notable change is the addition of a sentence on how to access the device's Hardware Property Manager service and a note on not all hardware properties are available on all devices.

entries.title: Network activity logging
entries.summary: The documentation has been updated to include information on network logging, including the types of events logged (DNS lookups and network connections), availability, and user notifications. The updates improve clarity by reformatting text and adding emphasis using callouts.

entries.title: Networking and telephony
entries.summary: This document underwent several changes. The changes include updating the links to the Android developer documentation, updating the table of features, and modifying some wording and formatting.

entries.title: RCS Google Messages archival
entries.summary: This document describes changes to the RCS Messages Archival documentation. It includes updates to the solution overview, workflow, implementation details (including Google Messages MCM schema, notification to archival app, and archival app requirements), and testing procedures. The changes clarify existing information and improve readability, and add an important note regarding syncing of every message event.

entries.title: Security
entries.summary: This document introduces changes to the security documentation, primarily involving the addition of the > [!NOTE] and > [!WARNING] blocks to highlight important information and the adb command to force security logs for testing purposes. These changes aim to enhance the clarity and emphasis of critical security aspects within the documentation.

entries.title: Manage system updates
entries.summary: This document introduces changes related to system update management for Android devices. The updates include clarifications and refinements to existing content, improved code examples, and adjustments to table contents to accurately reflect the functionality available to device and profile owners. The changes aim to enhance clarity and provide more accurate information regarding system update policies, callbacks, and freeze periods, improving the developer's understanding of DPC capabilities.

entries.title: What's new for Android in the enterprise
entries.summary: The documentation underwent several formatting changes, including adding line breaks for readability and updating links. These changes improve the overall presentation and clarity of the document, making it easier to understand the new features for each Android version in an enterprise setting.

entries.title: What's new for enterprise in Android 10
entries.summary: This document outlines changes in Android 10 related to enterprise features. Key updates include improved provisioning tools for work profiles, device-ID attestation for work profiles, and enhancements for work profile management such as device-wide blocking of app installations from unknown sources.

entries.title: What's new in Android 7.0
entries.summary: This document outlines the new features and API changes in Android 7.0 (API level 25) related to Android enterprise functionality. These updates enhance security, device management, and user experience within a work profile environment.

entries.title: What's new in Android 7.0
entries.summary: Key changes include support for QR code provisioning, a work profile security challenge, the ability to disable app access, work mode toggling, always-on VPN configuration, improved contact integration, remote reboot capabilities, a location off switch, customized provisioning options, multiple Wi-Fi CA certificate support, customized lock screen messages, and work profile connection services.

entries.title: What's new in Android 7.0
entries.summary: These changes improve enterprise device management by providing administrators with more control over devices, enhancing security with features like work profile challenges and always-on VPN, and improving the user experience with features like contact integration and the work mode toggle. This enables more secure and manageable corporate environments.

entries.title: What's new in Android 8.0
entries.summary: Android 8.0 introduces significant enhancements for enterprise Android deployments. Key changes include the ability to create work profiles on fully managed devices, enabling better separation of work and personal data. Additionally, user affiliation features allow for more device-wide control and functionality based on organizational identity. Several security improvements are also included, such as enhanced password reset and network logging capabilities.

entries.title: What’s in Android 9 for enterprise apps
entries.summary: This document outlines the enterprise-focused changes in Android 9. Key updates include UI enhancements for work profiles, APIs for cross-profile app switching, and expanded lock task mode capabilities. These changes aim to improve user experience and control in managed device scenarios.

entries.title: get.md
entries.summary: This document provides instructions on how to obtain and set up Android 10 for testing and development. The changes primarily involve formatting updates for better readability, and minor clarifications within the existing content. No significant new information or functional changes are introduced.

entries.title: setup-sdk.md
entries.summary: The documentation provides instructions on how to set up the Android 10 SDK in Android Studio, including getting the latest Android Studio, installing the SDK, and updating the build configuration. The changes primarily involve formatting and minor wording adjustments for better readability, such as adding line breaks and clarifying instructions. These changes help developers easily set up their environment and test Android 10 features and behavior changes.

entries.title: get.md
entries.summary: This document provides updated instructions and links for obtaining Android 12, including flashing, emulators, GSIs, and Android TV. The changes primarily refine the wording, improve clarity, and update links related to Android 12 resources. No significant feature changes were made.

entries.title: qrscanner.md
entries.summary: This document describes the Android 13 feature that adds a dedicated QR code scanner accessible from the Quick Settings tile to facilitate UPI payment flows. The changes include adding images, clarifying the behavior of the disambiguation dialog when multiple UPI apps are installed, and explaining how payment apps can identify the source of the QR code (live camera or static image) using intent extras.

entries.title: get.md
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to reflect minor formatting adjustments, improved clarity, and updated links for obtaining and setting up Android 13, including Pixel devices, emulators, and GSIs.
